stages.label: Stage 1
stages.description: During the start of the Tour, ASO is introducing something new. As has happened several times throughout history, the race will begin with a team time trial. But this time, it is not the time of the fifth finisher that counts—the time will be recorded individually for each rider. This makes the 19-kilometer time trial through Barcelona immediately interesting. In the final phase, the route becomes hectic and challenging as the riders climb Montjuïc from Plaza de España (1.1 kilometers at 5.1%). Once at the top, a final stretch of 800 meters at an average gradient of around 7 percent awaits. Team Visma | Lease a Bike Netcompany INEOS Red Bull - BORA - hansgrohe Decathlon CMA CGM Team Lidl - Trek UAE Team Emirates XRG Alpecin - Premier Tech EF Education - EasyPost Movistar Team Team Jayco AlUla Stage 1 Barcelona - Barcelona (19.7km) The Grand Départ of the Tour de France will take place in 2026 in Barcelona. After San Sebastian in 1992 and Bilbao in 2023, this will be only the third time that Spain hosts a Grand Départ. This year, the Tour will remain in Catalonia for three days before heading over the Pyrenees to France.

roadbook.location: Carrer de Jaume Vicens I Vives
Point chrono n°1 / Carrer de Llull
Avigunda de la Reina Maria Cristina
Point chrono n°3 / Passeig de Santa Madrona
Côte du Stade Olympique de Montjuïc

stages.label: Stage 2
stages.description: The stage begins on Sunday in the ancient Roman port city of Tarragona, after which the riders cover the first 90 kilometers along the coast before heading into the Catalan interior. There the climbs begin, starting halfway through the stage with the Côte de Begues (6.1 kilometers at 6.5%). After this climb, there is a brief lull, but with just over fifty kilometers to go, the race—at least in terms of climbing—bursts open. First comes the longer ascent of the Côte de Santa Creu d’Olorda (8.4 kilometers at 4.5%), followed by real leg-breakers on a local circuit in Barcelona that will cause plenty of pain. On that local circuit, the Côte du Château de Montjuïc (1.3 kilometers at 9.3%) and the Côte du Stade Olympique (600 meters at 7%) must be conquered three times, and it is very likely that the first of these climbs, with its steep gradients, will create casualties. Especially since the summit of that climb comes only 2.3 kilometers before the finish line.

roadbook.location: C-246a Racó de Santa Llúcia (VILANOVA I LA GELTRÚ)
Fontaines de Montjuïc - Entrée sur le circuit final
Stade Olympique - 1er passage sur la ligne d'arrivée
Stade Olympique - 2e passage sur la ligne d'arrivée

stages.label: Stage 3
stages.description: Monday’s third stage still starts in Granollers, a small town north of Barcelona. Over gently rolling roads, the peloton heads toward the Pyrenees. Early in the stage, the Sant Feliu de Codines (7.6 kilometers at 4.5%) must be climbed. The ascent is not too difficult, but it is an ideal moment for an early breakaway to gain a solid lead over the peloton. Only in the second half of the stage does the climbing resume. After nearly 120 kilometers, the riders begin the toughest climb of the day on paper: the Collada de Toses (9.3 kilometers at 6.5%). After a long descent, the riders reach France for the final part of the stage. Awaiting the peloton is the Col du Calvaire, an uncategorized but long climb of 14.9 kilometers. With gradients around 3 to 4 percent, this climb should not cause major damage. In the final phase of the stage, the riders climb toward Lac de Matemale, where the final ascent to Les Angles (1.7 kilometers at 7.6%) begins. In these uphill kilometers, explosive riders may still be able to force something.

roadbook.location: Côte de Saint Feliu de Codines
C-17 Tunnel de Sant Quirze de Besora
C-17 Tunnel de la Farga de Bebié (LES LLOSSES)

stages.label: Stage 4
stages.description: Stage four is a true transitional stage, the kind you would normally see only in the second or third week of the Tour de France. It is far too difficult for sprinters, while GC contenders will certainly not make their move here yet. So it will likely be a day for the breakaway. In the first hours of racing, there will be few opportunities to really get away. Yes, the Col de Villerouge and the Col de Bedos are on the route, but these climbs never really get steeper than four or five percent. The same goes for the Col du Paradis after about sixty kilometers. After halfway through the race, the real climbs and mountain points follow. After nearly 100 kilometers, the riders begin the ascent of the Col de Coudons (10.7 kilometers at 5.5%), where we will undoubtedly see a strong battle among the breakaway riders. After this climb, the riders remain on a plateau for quite some time before descending for about thirty kilometers toward Bélesta.

roadbook.location: D16 Col de la Croix des Morts

stages.label: Stage 5
stages.description: The stage is only 158 kilometers long and makes a northern loop around the Pyrenees, largely through the Gers Valley. When the riders reach Vic-en-Bigorre, there are still 45 kilometers left to the finish in Pau. Here, three small climbs follow within twelve kilometers: the Côte de Casteide-Doat (1.5 kilometers at 5.1%), Côte de Flancart (1 kilometer at 6.4%), and Côte de Baleix (1.3 kilometers at 7%). After that last climb, 26 flat kilometers remain to the finish in Pau, where a Tour stage will end for the 64th time in history. The last time the Tour finished in Pau was in 2024, when Jasper Philipsen took the victory. Tim Merlier Olav Kooij Jasper Philipsen Arnaud De Lie Biniam Girmay Hailu Mads Pedersen Pascal Ackermann Pavel Bittner Milan Fretin Jordi Meeus Stage 5 Lannemezan - Pau (158.3km) After four days of racing through the Catalan hills and the Pyrenees, the sprinters will finally get their first mass sprint on day five—at least, that is the expectation. The stage takes the riders from Lannemezan to Pau.

stages.label: Stage 6
stages.description: After the start in Pau, a calm and classic approach toward the Pyrenees awaits. Via Lourdes and Bagnères-de-Bigorre, the riders continue to Arreau, where the foot of the first Pyrenean giant of the day lies: the Col d’Aspin (12 kilometers at 6.5%). On the profile chart, the Aspin may look like a speed bump compared to what follows, but the climb is truly tough—especially because its steepest kilometers consistently rise above 9 percent. After the Aspin, the race descends to Sainte-Marie-de-Campan, where the ascent of the biggest mountain of the day begins: the mythical Col du Tourmalet (17 kilometers at 7.3%). The longer the Tourmalet goes on, the steeper it becomes. From the tenth kilometer onward, the gradient barely drops below nine percent. At 2,115 meters, a mountain sprint for the Souvenir Jacques Goddet awaits, after which 39 kilometers remain to the finish. The final 39 kilometers of the stage are not overly difficult, although… After a 22-kilometer descent, one more climb of 18 kilometers with an average gradient of 3.7 percent remains. At this point, it would be very useful for a GC rider to still have a teammate by their side.

roadbook.location: D938 Quartier Haut de la Côte
Col du Tourmalet - Souvenir Jacques Goddet

stages.label: Stage 7
stages.description: This Friday’s stage starts in the little-known town of Hagetmau. This small village of just 5,000 inhabitants has never before hosted the Tour de France, so it will certainly be a big celebration. After the start, the riders will quickly head north toward Bordeaux. There won’t be much excitement along the way, as the roads are mostly flat. On the way to the sprinter city of Bordeaux, the only climb is the Côte de Béguey (1.6 kilometers at 4.3%), located 40 kilometers from the finish, but no climber is expected to be dropped here. So a ‘regular’ sprint is expected in Bordeaux. The capital of the Gironde has already hosted a Tour stage 133 times, and in the past it has predominantly been sprinters who won here. Think of Jasper Philipsen in 2023 or Mark Cavendish in 2010. In the past, Dutch riders have also won here regularly. Notable examples include Cees Priem, Bert Oosterbosch, Jan Raas, and Jean-Paul van Poppel in the 1980s, and of course Servais Knaven in 2003. He is the last Dutchman to win here.

stages.label: Stage 8
stages.description: Saturday’s route is slightly more varied than Friday’s. In the first eighty kilometers, the riders head east toward Vézac, before continuing through the Dordogne Valley toward Bergerac. Tim Merlier Olav Kooij Jasper Philipsen Arnaud De Lie Biniam Girmay Hailu Mads Pedersen Pascal Ackermann Pavel Bittner Milan Fretin Jordi Meeus Stage 8 Périgueux - Bergerac (180.4km) The sprinters who messed up their sprint in Bordeaux on Friday will immediately have a chance to redeem themselves on day eight of the Tour de France. On Saturday, the riders face an almost entirely flat 182-kilometer stage from Périgueux to Bergerac. Saturday’s route is slightly more varied than Friday’s. In the first eighty kilometers, the riders head east toward Vézac, before continuing through the Dordogne Valley toward Bergerac.

roadbook.location: D46 E Carrefour D46 E-D50-D46 E3

stages.label: Stage 9
stages.description: The start takes place Sunday in Malemort. Late in the morning, the riders set off on a 185-kilometer stage. The terrain rolls up and down all day as the riders wind their way north, with uncategorized climbs such as the Puy Boubou (2.8 kilometers at 4.1%), Côte de Lagleygeolle (5.2 kilometers at 3.9%), and Côte de Miel (6.6 kilometers at 3.9%). Midway through the stage, the Côte de Naves (2.8 kilometers at 6.7%) and the Puy de Lachaud (3.6 kilometers at 5.3%) follow. After 105 kilometers, the Suc au May (3.9 kilometers at 7.7%) is the only categorized climb of the day. In the final part of the race, several tough climbs still await. Consider the Mont Bessou (800 meters at 8.5%), located 24 kilometers from the finish, climbing at around eight percent for a full kilometer. The final hill is the Côte des Gardes (2.2 kilometers at 4.8%), 14 kilometers from the finish line.

roadbook.location: D128 E Col du Bos (D128 E-D128)
Côte de la Croix du Pey

stages.label: Stage 10
stages.description: In this tenth stage, the peloton travels 167 kilometers through the Massif Central. The riders start in the afternoon in Aurillac, followed by a flat opening phase. Only in the final eighty kilometers does the vast majority of the 3,900 meters of climbing appear. After nearly 100 kilometers, the riders reach the top of the first official climb of the day: the Col de la Griffoul (5.9 kilometers at 6.7%). From here on, the stage contains no flat sections at all. There are the ascents of the Col de Prat de Bouc and the Côte de Murat, but no mountain points are awarded at their summits. After nearly 100 kilometers, the riders crest the first official climb, the Col de la Griffoul—5.9 kilometers at 6.7%. Soon after comes the Col de Prat de Bouc (3.2 kilometers at 5.8%), and directly from the descent, the Côte de Murat (6.6 kilometers at 4.4%). Strictly speaking, these are nothing more than preliminary skirmishes.

roadbook.location: VC Col de Font de Cère

stages.label: Stage 11
stages.description: There is little excitement along the way on Wednesday. After leaving Vichy, the riders follow the Allier River for a long stretch. Near Moulins, they then head toward the Loire Valley. Once they have crossed it, they take a detour into Nevers. A sprint will then decide the stage. This will be the fourth time the Tour has finished in Nevers: in the past, winners here have included Eric Leman, Guido Bontempo, and Alessandro Petacchi. Jasper Philipsen Olav Kooij Mads Pedersen Arnaud De Lie Biniam Girmay Hailu Kaden Groves Pascal Ackermann Milan Fretin Michael Matthews Tim Merlier Stage 11 Vichy - Nevers (161.3km) The sprinters get another chance at stage victory in the second week. The 161 kilometers between Vichy and Nevers are almost entirely flat, so few riders will entertain the illusion of preventing a bunch sprint.

roadbook.location: D979 A Champ des Balais (AVERMES, YZEURE)

stages.label: Stage 12
stages.description: From the circuit, the riders head east on Thursday. As in previous stages, they cross the Loire before eventually reaching the Morvan Nature Park. Here awaits the only obstacle of the day: the Côte de Montagny-lès-Buxy, a climb of just 2.6 kilometers with an average gradient of 3.9 percent. Will climbers really be dropped here? We can hardly imagine it. Therefore, the peloton will spend the final twenty kilometers preparing for a mass sprint. Jasper Philipsen Olav Kooij Mads Pedersen Arnaud De Lie Biniam Girmay Hailu Kaden Groves Pascal Ackermann Milan Fretin Jordi Meeus Tim Merlier Stage 12 Circuit Nevers Magny-Cours - Châlon-sur-Saône (179.1km) Thursday is another day for the sprinters! After twelve days of racing, it will already be the fifth opportunity for a bunch sprint in the Tour de France. Between the Circuit de Nevers Magny-Cours and Chalon-sur-Saône, there are hardly any elevation changes.

stages.label: Stage 13
stages.description: The thirteenth stage covers a total of 205 kilometers, making it the longest stage in this Tour de France. It is also the only stage longer than 200 kilometers. Before any real climbing begins, the riders must cover roughly 150 kilometers northeast from the start in Dole. The first climb comes only after 150 kilometers, over the Col des Croix (5.4 kilometers at 4.9%). No major casualties are expected here, but the next climb will likely make a difference. The Tour revisits the Ballon d’Alsace (8.7 kilometers at 6.9%), which, 121 years ago, was the first real climb in Tour history—a moment that will surely be commemorated this Friday. The Ballon d’Alsace is a steady climb, but it still has a peak section of 8.8 percent along the way.

stages.label: Stage 14
stages.description: From Mulhouse, the riders quickly reach the foot of the first climb of the day: the Grand Ballon (21.5 kilometers at 4.8%). Although the climb can be approached from seven different sides, it is actually tougher than it looks on paper. This is because there are some flat and descending sections within the climb. Remove those, and about fifteen kilometers remain at gradients of 7 to 8 percent. After the Grand Ballon, the riders descend to the first passage of the finish in Le Markstein, followed by a middle section including the Col du Page (9.8 kilometers at 4.7%) and once again the Ballon d’Alsace (9.8 kilometers at 6.9%). After these climbs, the stage awaits its final ascent. The Col du Schirm and Col du Hundsruck must still be climbed, but they pale in comparison to what comes at the finish.

roadbook.location: D27 LE MARKSTEIN FELLERING - 1er passage sur la ligne d'arrivée
D13 BIS KRUTH (D13 BIS-D13 BIS I)

stages.label: Stage 15
stages.description: The stage starts on Sunday in Champagnole, in the Jura. From this picturesque village—surrounded by plenty of greenery along the Ain—the riders begin climbing almost immediately. They tackle the Mont Rivel right away, a climb 22 kilometers long but with an average gradient of only 2.1 percent. After this ascent comes a short descent leading to the Côte de Doppes (15.2 kilometers at 3.6%). This climb is also very long but again has a relatively low gradient. Next follows a descent of roughly 50 kilometers with some short climbs, until about 60 kilometers from the finish. Here, the first truly serious climb appears: the Col de la Croissette (7.6 kilometers at 8.8%), after which approximately fifty kilometers remain to the finish. In the finale, the Plateau de Solaison (11.3 kilometers at 9.1%) awaits as a beast of a final climb. This ascent, which was also featured a few years ago in the Dauphiné, hits hard immediately with gradients over ten percent. After that, the gradient oscillates between 8 and 10 percent all the way to the summit.

roadbook.location: D45 La Croisette (D45-D41 A-D215) (LA MURAZ, COLLONGES-SOUS-SALÈVE)
Le Salève - Col de la Croisette

stages.label: Stage 16
stages.description: The time trial takes the riders along the lake from Évian-les-Bains to Thonon-les-Bains and includes approximately 500 meters of elevation gain. This is due to a hill immediately after the start: a climb 9.6 kilometers long with an average gradient of 4.2 percent. After seven kilometers, the course descends back down to the lake. From the lake, nine kilometers remain to the finish in Thonon-les-Bains. This time trial could once again shake up the general classification significantly. Remco Evenepoel Tadej Pogačar Jonas Vingegaard Juan Ayuso Pesquera Florian Lipowitz Kévin Vauquelin Thymen Arensman Bruno Armirail Mathias Vacek Mathieu van der Poel Stage 16 Évian-les-Bains - Thonon-les-Bains (26.1km) After the Jura, the Tour riders head quickly to the Alps, where the big showdown of the Tour de France is set to take place. On the Tuesday following the second rest day, a 26-kilometer time trial awaits at Lake Geneva, specifically in Thonon-les-Bains.

roadbook.location: Point chrono n°2 / Côte de Larringes
Point chrono n°3 / THONON-LES-BAINS-Bords de Dranse

stages.label: Stage 17
stages.description: After a flat opening from Chambéry, the riders climb for about thirty kilometers after roughly twenty kilometers of racing. Nowhere is it truly steep, as the highest point of the stage is only around 1,100 meters: the Col des Prés. Only the final kilometers are somewhat challenging, with a 3.5-kilometer stretch averaging 6.6 percent. This is followed by a descent back to Chambéry and the Col de Couz (8.6 kilometers at 2.8%). This hill is the last real obstacle before the finish, which is still 85 kilometers away. Nowhere in the last two hours is it entirely flat, but it is far from difficult. The final 35 kilometers even mostly descend. In the finale, a small hill of 2.5 kilometers at 3.5 percent awaits—but will it be enough to prevent a sprint in Voiron? So late in the Tour de France, you never really know.

roadbook.location: D82 M Les Éteppes-La Gare (LE PONT-DE-BEAUVOISIN) (D82 M-D82)
Vers Ars (D50-D17) (VILLAGES DU LAC DE PALADRU)

stages.label: Stage 18
stages.description: After the start, the riders head south toward Grenoble, where the first climb of the day awaits: the Engins (11.4 kilometers at 5.4%). After a long plateau section, the peloton briefly enters the valley. Midway through the stage, the climb to Monteynard (9.7 kilometers at 5.2%) follows. After this climb, the riders encounter a long plateau. Only 32 kilometers from the finish does the climbing resume, though still at gentle gradients. The real challenge begins just seven kilometers from the finish with the ascent to Orcières-Merlette (7.1 kilometers at 6.8%). Thymen Arensman Richard Carapaz Lenny Martinez Sepp Kuss Tom Pidcock Mattias Skjelmose Giulio Ciccone Ben Healy Ben O'Connor Michael Storer Stage 18 Voiron - Orcières-Merlette (185.2km) The Alps had been looming on the horizon in the Tour de France, but on Thursday, it is finally time: the first true Alpine stage, with a total of 3,800 meters of climbing. The stage starts in the morning from Voiron, covering 185 kilometers to the ski resort of Orcières-Merlette.

stages.label: Stage 19
stages.description: The start, as has often been the case in Tour history, is in Gap. From Gap, the riders face a 128-kilometer stage with a total of four climbs. The first comes immediately after the start: the Col de Bayard (5.1 kilometers at 7.2%). Directly after the descent, the Col du Noyer (7.2 kilometers at 8.5%) follows. The peloton will already be considerably thinned out after this tough opening phase—only 25 kilometers have been covered! After these two climbs, there is a long period of relative calm. Only 75 kilometers later does climbing resume with the Col d’Ornon (5.4 kilometers at 6.4%), signaling the approach to the finale. After a 13-kilometer descent, the riders arrive in Bourg d’Oisans. From there, you know what’s coming: Alpe d’Huez (13.8 kilometers at 8.1%) begins!

roadbook.location: D212 B Les Miards (D212 B-D26 A) (SAINT-LAURENT-EN-BEAUMONT)

stages.label: Stage 20
stages.description: The stage starts on this Saturday in Bourg d’Oisans. This town lies at the foot of both Alpe d’Huez and the Col de la Croix de Fer (24 kilometers at 5.2%). The riders tackle the Croix de Fer first, reaching an altitude of over 2,000 meters after just 34 kilometers of racing. After the summit of the Croix de Fer, the riders descend toward Saint-Jean-de-Maurienne, signaling the upcoming combination of Col du Télégraphe and Col du Galibier. The Télégraphe (11.9 kilometers at 7.1%) may seem like a minor obstacle compared to the other climbs in this stage, but it will greatly increase fatigue among the riders. Especially since there is no descent after this climb—it almost immediately continues into the Col du Galibier (17.7 kilometers at 6.9%). At 2,642 meters, this is instantly the highest point of the Tour. After the Galibier, there are still 61 kilometers remaining in the stage.

roadbook.location: Col de la Croix de Fer
Col du Galibier - Souvenir Henri Desgrange

stages.label: Stage 21
stages.description: The approach to Paris will be leisurely, with the riders covering roughly fifty kilometers from Thoiry while chatting, taking photos, and joking with one another. With just under 80 kilometers remaining, the peloton enters Paris, and the racing truly begins. The stage starts with three laps of 6.8 kilometers around and along the Champs-Élysées, followed by three more laps of a 16.7-kilometer finishing circuit. Each of these laps features the Côte de la Butte Montmartre (1.1 kilometers at 5.9%), with the final ascent coming 6.1 kilometers from the finish. Whether there will be a sprint is still uncertain. In 2025—when Montmartre was first included in the Tour—the climb provided plenty of excitement. On a soaking wet course, five riders broke away from a leading group in the finale, with Wout van Aert managing to drop Tadej Pogačar. The Slovenian, however, secured the yellow jersey soon after.

roadbook.location: D181 Côte du Pavé des Gardes

stages.label: Stage 1
stages.description: The opening stage of the Arctic Race of Norway takes the peloton on a 181.7-kilometre journey from Evenes to Myre. With a total elevation gain of around 2,200 metres, it promises to be a demanding day in the saddle. Several climbs are featured along the route, including Kåringen and Storheia, offering opportunities for attackers and strong climbers to make their move. Despite the hilly terrain, a reduced bunch sprint in Myre remains a realistic outcome.

stages.label: Stage 2
stages.description: The second stage of the Arctic Race of Norway covers 179.2 kilometres from Bø to Andenes. With approximately 1,300 metres of elevation gain, it is less demanding than the opening stage, although several short climbs and gravel sections add an extra layer of difficulty. The run-in to Andenes is relatively flat, making it likely that a reduced peloton will battle for victory in a bunch sprint. However, the wind could play a crucial role and significantly influence the outcome of the race.

stages.label: Stage 3
stages.description: At 142.5 kilometres, the third stage of the Arctic Race of Norway is relatively short but highly explosive, featuring 1,800 metres of elevation gain. After tackling the Storvatnet climb twice, the riders face a brutal finale on Storheia, where the final ascent measures 3.5 kilometres at an average gradient of 11.8%. This mountaintop finish is expected to create significant time gaps and offers climbers and general classification contenders an ideal opportunity to make their mark.

stages.label: Stage 4
stages.description: The final stage of the Arctic Race of Norway takes the riders from Sortland to Narvik over a challenging 190.8 kilometres. With an impressive 2,400 metres of elevation gain, it is the toughest stage of the race. Several classified climbs await throughout the day, before the decisive ascent of Narvikfjellet, a steep 2.3-kilometre climb averaging 8.6%. This uphill finish provides the perfect opportunity for the general classification contenders to make one last move and could prove decisive in determining the overall winner of the Arctic Race of Norway.

stages.label: Stage 1
stages.description: The 2026 Tour of Austria begins with a varied opening stage from Graz to Gamlitz. Over 188 kilometres and more than 2,700 metres of climbing, the riders immediately face a serious challenge. The rolling route through Styria features several tough climbs and short explosive hills, making it ideal terrain for attackers and punchers. The finale around Gamlitz promises plenty of action, with opportunities for a reduced sprint or a late attack to claim the first leader’s jersey.

stages.label: Stage 2
stages.description: The second stage of the 2026 Tour of Austria heads straight into the mountains. From Bad Kleinkirchheim to the legendary finish at the Franz Josef Höhe, the riders face a demanding stage of nearly 189 kilometres with more than 2,900 metres of climbing. After a long approach, a brutal final ascent towards the Grossglockner awaits, where the general classification contenders will have to show their true form for the first time. A first major test for the climbers and an immediate opportunity to make a difference in the overall standings.

stages.label: Stage 3
stages.description: The third stage of the 2026 Tour of Austria takes the peloton from Lienz to St. Johann Alpendorf. With nearly 189 kilometres and more than 2,300 metres of climbing, the riders once again face a tough Alpine stage. After two demanding climbs in the opening phase, a long and fast middle section follows, but the finale features more challenging hills towards the finish. A stage that offers opportunities for attackers, while the GC contenders will need to stay alert to avoid losing valuable seconds.

stages.label: Stage 4
stages.description: Stage four of the 2026 Tour of Austria takes place on a hilly course around Steyr. Over 170 kilometres and more than 2,300 metres of climbing, the riders will have little time to recover. The succession of short climbs and technical roads makes this stage ideal for attackers and punchers. In the local finale around Steyr, the race could unfold in many different ways, promising plenty of spectacle and an intense battle for victory.

stages.label: Stage 5
stages.description: The final stage of the 2026 Tour of Austria takes the peloton from Langenlois to Vienna. At 109 kilometres, it is the shortest stage of this edition, but the riders certainly cannot afford to underestimate it. Several punchy climbs in the first half and a tricky hill towards the finale could still have a major impact on the race. Nevertheless, the stage appears likely to end in a battle between the sprinters, who will have one final chance in the Austrian capital to conclude their Tour with a stage victory.

stages.description: Due to the increased fire risk in the Mount Lofty Ranges and the forecast maximum temperature of 43 degrees, Stage 4 has been modified for safety reasons. The climbs of Willunga Hill have been removed, and the total distance has been reduced from 176 kilometres to 130.8 kilometres.

stages.label: Stage 1
stages.description: Juan Ayuso Pesquera Kévin Vauquelin Jonas Vingegaard Dorian Godon Oscar Onley Matteo Trentin Orluis Alberto Aular Sanabria Aurélien Paret-Peintre Iván Romeo Abad Mike Teunissen Stage 1 Achères - Carrières-sous-Poissy (171.0km) The 84th edition of Paris–Nice gets underway in Achères, in the Yvelines department. The opening stage promises immediate excitement, as the riders finish on a challenging circuit around the finish town of Carrières-sous-Poissy, with the demanding Côte de Chanteloup-les-Vignes (1.1 km at 8.3%) set to play a decisive role.

stages.label: Stage 2
stages.description: Biniam Girmay Hailu Milan Fretin Luke Lamperti Pascal Ackermann Phil Bauhaus Bryan Coquard Stage 2 Epône - Montargis (187.0km) In short, the puncheurs can immediately have their say, but the second stage from Épône to Montargis looks tailor-made for the sprinters, given the virtually flat run-in to the finish.

stages.label: Stage 3
stages.description: With the Grand Départ of the Tour de France in Barcelona in mind, stage three features a 23.5-kilometre team time trial between Cosne-Cours-sur-Loire and Pouilly-sur-Loire. This stage can therefore be seen as a dress rehearsal for the TTT in the upcoming Tour.

stages.label: Stage 4
stages.description: Jonas Vingegaard Juan Ayuso Pesquera Kévin Vauquelin Daniel Felipe Martinez Lenny Martinez Oscar Onley Stage 4 Bourges - Uchon (195.0km) The riders then head towards the roads of the Morvan. The fourth stage should not be underestimated, as the finale presents the riders with two climbs: the Côte de la Croix de la Libération (4.6 km at 5.3%) and the final ascent to Uchon (8 km at 4.5%).

stages.label: Stage 5
stages.description: Jonas Vingegaard Lenny Martinez Kévin Vauquelin David Gaudu Daniel Felipe Martinez Oscar Onley Stage 5 Cormoranche-sur-Saône - Colombier-le-Vieux (206.8km) The fifth stage, finishing in Colombier-le-Vieux, is also tough, featuring four steep climbs in the final roughly forty kilometres. In fact, the fifth stage boasts the highest total elevation gain of the race.

stages.label: Stage 6
stages.description: The sixth stage, taking place between Barbentane and Apt, could also create gaps in the general classification. Along the way, there are two third-category climbs and two second-category climbs. After the final categorized obstacle—the Côte de Saignon (4 km at 5%)—there are just over four kilometres of descent to the finish.

stages.label: Stage 7
stages.description: The stage was originally 138.7 kilometers long and included 2,580 meters of elevation gain, but due to snow in the mountains the stage had to be significantly shortened, reducing the queen stage to 47 kilometers. In the final weekend, the battle for the overall victory will truly ignite. The penultimate stage heads to the ski resort of Auron, where the finish line comes after a 7.3-kilometre climb averaging just over 7%. This year, the French race also reached Auron, where Michael Storer managed to solo to victory in near-apocalyptic conditions.

stages.label: Stage 8
stages.description: Jonas Vingegaard Lenny Martinez Kévin Vauquelin Daniel Felipe Martinez Valentin Paret-Peintre Harold Alfonso Tejada Canacue Stage 8 Nice - Nice (128.8km) The eighth and final stage will— as previously mentioned—finish in Nice, but not on the well-known Promenade des Anglais. The race organizers will also bypass the usual decisive climbs in and around Nice, including the Col d’Èze. The action is expected mainly on the slopes of the Col de la Porte (7 km at 7.2%), the Côte de Châteauneuf-Villevieille (6.6 km at 6.6%), or the relatively unknown Côte de Linguador (3.3 km at 8.8%).

stages.label: Stage 1
stages.description: The riders leave Nesebar quickly and spend virtually the entire day tracing the Black Sea coastline. After 60 kilometres, a loop around Lake Burgas introduces the only categorised climb of the day — a gentle rise to just 77 metres. One of the day's breakaway riders will claim the first mountain jersey here. After the sole KOM sprint, the race swings south along the coast before curving back into Burgas for a wide-open bunch sprint. Who grabs the first maglia rosa? Jonathan Milan Dylan Groenewegen Paul Magnier Tobias Lund Andresen Kaden Groves Casper van Uden Erlend Blikra Arnaud De Lie Matteo Malucelli Ethan Vernon Stage 1 Nessebar - Burgas (146.9km) The 2026 Giro d'Italia begins with a historic Grande Partenza in Bulgaria — the first time an Eastern European country has ever hosted a Grand Tour opening. On Friday 8 May, the sprinters get their first opportunity to pull on the pink jersey as the peloton rolls over flat roads from Nesebar to Burgas.

stages.label: Stage 2
stages.description: At 220 kilometres, this is one of the longest stages in the race. The peloton rolls out of Burgas — Bulgaria's fourth-largest city, best known for its vast port — and almost immediately heads inland. The first hundred kilometres run in a straight westerly line before the race encounters its first serious altitude. After 117 and 135 kilometres, the Byala Pass and the Vratnik Pass — topping out at 920 metres — demand respect. The decisive climb, however, comes in the finale. With 13 kilometres to go, the road kicks up towards the Lyaskovets Monastery: 3.5 kilometres at an average of 7.5 per cent. That is steep enough to create genuine gaps. A lone attacker might even arrive solo into Veliko Tarnovo. Corbin Strong Jan Christen Andrea Vendrame Tobias Lund Andresen Christian Scaroni Ben Turner Orluis Alberto Aular Sanabria Kaden Groves Guillermo Thomas Silva Coussan Edoardo Zambanini Stage 2 Burgas - Veliko Tarnovo (221.4km) The second stage is considerably more demanding. Three third-category climbs punctuate the route and the puncheurs will fancy their chances of taking the pink jersey from whoever sprinted to glory the day before.

stages.label: Stage 3
stages.description: The stage begins in Bulgaria's second-largest city and heads west towards Sofia at pace. The opening 60 kilometres are straightforward, offering early breakaway riders plenty of room to move. The road then starts to rise — gradually at first, with gradients around one to two per cent. After 93 kilometres, the Borovets Pass (9.3 km at 5.6%) begins in earnest. It is the hardest and longest ascent of the entire Bulgarian opening, though manageable enough that a determined sprinter can limit their losses. The summit leaves 71 kilometres to the finish, mostly downhill — plenty of time for dropped riders to return to the front group. The finish in Sofia marks the first time a major professional cycling race has visited the Bulgarian capital. After the stage, riders board flights to Italy, where the Giro resumes on Tuesday.

stages.label: Stage 4
stages.description: The fourth stage departs Catanzaro on flat roads through the Calabrian landscape before the climbing begins around the 80-kilometre mark. The Cozzo Tunno is a 15-kilometre drag to above 900 metres, averaging 5.9 per cent — demanding but not brutal. With 43 kilometres remaining after the summit, the question is whether dropped riders can claw their way back. A breakaway survivor might even hold on to take the stage win in Cosenza. A new race leader? Filippo Ganna Guillermo Thomas Silva Coussan Jasper Stuyven Simone Gualdi Christian Scaroni Ben Turner Tobias Lund Andresen Orluis Alberto Aular Sanabria Jhonnatan Manuel Narváez Corbin Strong Stage 4 Catanzaro - Cosenza (138.8km) After a rest day, the Giro resumes in the deep south of Italy. The peloton rolls through Calabria with a sting in the tail.

stages.label: Stage 5
stages.description: This is the third-longest stage of the race. The start is in Praia a Mare on the Tyrrhenian coast — Sam Bennett won a Giro stage here back in 2018 — but the chances of a sprinter repeating that feat are virtually zero. Despite only two categorised climbs on the profile, the road barely levels out all day. The first climb comes almost immediately after the flag drops: the Prestieri (13 km at 4%), where any early break will find room to breathe. Then comes the main course: the Montagna Grande di Viggiano (17 km at 4.7%), a second-category ascent with several ramps above ten per cent. More than 40 kilometres remain after the summit. The finale is littered with uncategorised rises. Five kilometres from the line, riders must clear a 2.3-kilometre bump at 5.8 per cent. A day for the breakaway.

stages.label: Stage 6
stages.description: In the finale, riders complete a loop around Vesuvius and enter Naples from the east. In the Fuorigrotta district, a namesake climb (2 km at 6.2%) is designed to shake up the sprinters. Nine flat kilometres remain after the summit. Will the pure sprinters survive? Paul Magnier Dylan Groenewegen Jonathan Milan Tobias Lund Andresen Madis Mihkels Ethan Vernon Orluis Alberto Aular Sanabria Matteo Malucelli Ben Turner Casper van Uden Stage 6 Paestum - Napoli (141.4km) The Giro has made Naples a near-annual fixture in recent years and 2026 is no different. Stage six arrives at the famous port city — though whether it ends in a bunch sprint is not entirely certain. In the finale, riders complete a loop around Vesuvius and enter Naples from the east. In the Fuorigrotta district, a namesake climb (2 km at 6.2%) is designed to shake up the sprinters. Nine flat kilometres remain after the summit. Will the pure sprinters survive?

stages.label: Stage 7
stages.description: The opening 140 kilometres from Formia are predominantly flat as the peloton threads its way into the Apennines. The road gets harder from Colli al Volturno onwards, with the uncategorised Rionero Sannitico (9.5 km at 5%) lifting riders above 1,000 metres. The Roccaraso (7 km at 6.4%) follows before the peloton spends roughly 50 kilometres on a high plateau between 1,000 and 1,200 metres. At the 200-kilometre mark the road plunges. After a 20-kilometre descent and 10 flat kilometres, riders hit the base of Blockhaus with 13.6 kilometres to go — averaging 8.4 per cent, at its most savage in the opening kilometres. The last time the Giro visited Blockhaus, Jai Hindley took the stage win. Nairo Quintana won here the year Tom Dumoulin claimed overall victory. Franco Pellizotti triumphed on these slopes in 2009.

stages.label: Stage 8
stages.description: From Chieti, the peloton swings quickly toward the Adriatic coast and follows it for a considerable stretch. The real fireworks start at Cupra Marittima, where the road turns inland and the lumpy terrain begins. The Montefiore dell'Aso is followed by the Monterubbiano (4.7 km at 5.8%), with fewer than 40 kilometres remaining after that summit. The route actually passes through the finish town of Fermo once before the end, where an uncategorised wall — 500 metres at 11 per cent through the cobbled streets of this medieval hilltop town — will scatter the bunch. After the top, a 25-kilometre loop down to the coast precedes the decisive climb back up: 3.2 kilometres averaging 6.2 per cent, with ramps above ten per cent from the very start. The last time a race finished in Fermo was Tirreno-Adriatico 2022, when Warren Barguil escaped from the breakaway to take the win. A similar scenario is very much on the cards.

stages.label: Stage 9
stages.description: The peloton rolls through Bologna and edges westward into the Apennines. A modest three-kilometre climb appears at the 107-kilometre mark, followed by roughly 50 more flat kilometres. Then the stage changes character entirely. The Querciola (9.7 km at 4.3%) serves as a warm-up, connected by a brief descent to the foot of the final climb: the Corno alle Scale, 12.8 kilometres at 5.9%. In combination with what comes before, the final 28 kilometres average 4.4 per cent — with the last three kilometres ramping up to an average of 9.9 per cent. Jonas Vingegaard Giulio Ciccone Lennert Van Eetvelt Jan Christen Jai Hindley Christian Scaroni Felix Gall Ben O'Connor Giulio Pellizzari Wout Poels Stage 9 Cervia - Corno alle Scale (183.9km) Stage nine is a near-classic uphill finish — what the Spanish would call a unipuerto stage. Ninety per cent of the 184 kilometres from the Adriatic resort of Cervia is flat, but what waits at the finish is anything but.

stages.label: Stage 10
stages.description: The start ramp is set up on the seafront promenade in Viareggio. The opening 15 kilometres head south via a slight detour along ruler-straight coastal roads, where riders will need to get up to speed immediately. After roughly 20 kilometres, the course turns inland and becomes more technical — rolling false-flat sections and short kinks that break the rhythm. Wide roads return in the closing stretch toward Massa. Crosswinds and a gently rising final kilometre could make the finish mentally as much as physically draining. Filippo Ganna Mikkel Bjerg Magnus Sheffield Thymen Arensman Niklas Larsen Lorenzo Milesi Rémi Cavagna Alec Segaert Matteo Sobrero Jonas Vingegaard Stage 10 Viareggio - Massa (42.0km) The general classification will be turned on its head during the tenth stage: a long individual time trial along the Tyrrhenian coast. Forty kilometres of full-gas effort awaits.

stages.label: Stage 11
stages.description: That is where the day gets serious. The climbs to La Foce (2.6 km at 6.8%), Pignone (2 km at 7.2%) and the Passo del Termine (7 km at 5.1%) set up a testing finale. The Colle di Guaitarola (9.7 km at 6.3%) can cause real selection, before the race threads back to the coast via Sestri Levante. Nine kilometres from the line in Chiavari — nestled on the Gulf of Tigullio not far from Genoa — the climb to San Bartolomeo (4.6 km at 6.4%) fires the final salvo. A fast descent brings the riders down to the finish. This stage is made for attackers and puncheurs. Giulio Ciccone Jhonnatan Manuel Narváez Lennert Van Eetvelt Igor Arrieta Lizarraga Jan Christen Einer Rubio Reyes Andreas Leknessund Christian Scaroni Michael Valgren Andersen Filippo Zana Stage 11 Porcari - Chiavari (195.4km) Wednesday's stage leaves Tuscany and heads for the Ligurian coast. From Porcari, the peloton follows the Tyrrhenian shoreline for a relaxed opening phase before the road turns inland toward La Spezia.

stages.label: Stage 12
stages.description: The Colle Giovo (7.8 km at 4.9%) and the Bric Berton (7.5 km at 4.9%) are the day's principal obstacles. Neither is particularly steep, but at race pace they can test even well-organised sprint trains. More than 50 flat kilometres follow the final climb, setting up a textbook cat-and-mouse finale on wide roads. Novi Ligure breathes cycling history. Caleb Ewan won a stage here in 2019. The profile points to puncheurs with fast legs. Paul Magnier Orluis Alberto Aular Sanabria Ben Turner Tobias Lund Andresen Jhonnatan Manuel Narváez Corbin Strong Alberto Bettiol Filippo Ganna Ivan Garcia Cortina Jasper Stuyven Stage 12 Imperia - Novi Ligure (175.5km) From Imperia on the Ligurian coast, the peloton heads inland through Piedmont on Thursday. The landscape rolls and glows — this is classic Italian cycling country.

stages.label: Stage 13
stages.description: Two short, sharp climbs await the riders: first Bieno (2.3 km at 6%), then Ungiasca (4.7 km at 7%) with ramps approaching ten per cent. The top of Ungiasca sits roughly 14 kilometres from the finish. A descent leads to the shores of Lake Maggiore and into Verbania — birthplace of Filippo Ganna. Philippe Gilbert won here with a late attack in 2015. The profile suits puncheurs, though an attacker who pushes clear on the final climb and holds firm on the descent is equally viable. Jhonnatan Manuel Narváez Giulio Ciccone Christian Scaroni Toon Aerts Guillermo Thomas Silva Coussan Diego Ulissi Orluis Alberto Aular Sanabria Jan Christen Filippo Ganna Jasper Stuyven Stage 13 Alessandria - Verbania (187.4km) Friday's stage covers 186 kilometres from Alessandria to Verbania on Lake Maggiore. The bulk of the route is flat, but the finale packs a punch.

stages.label: Stage 14
stages.description: The Col du Saint-Barthélemy (15.5 km at 6.9%) arrives early and announces the day's intentions. Doues (5.5 km at 6.5%), Lin Noir (7.5 km at 7.8%) and Verrogne (5.4 km at 7.2%) follow in relentless succession. The peloton will be shredded before the final climb even begins. That climb is Pila: 15.9 kilometres at 7.3% to a ski resort above Aosta with Mont Blanc on the horizon. Pure climbers will take control here. GC time can be made and lost. Jonas Vingegaard Thymen Arensman Felix Gall Giulio Ciccone Davide Piganzoli Einer Rubio Reyes Igor Arrieta Lizarraga Giulio Pellizzari Christian Scaroni Aleksander Vlasov Stage 14 Aosta - Pila (133.0km) Saturday is a mountain day in the Alps. Just 133 kilometres, but more than 4,000 metres of climbing — and not a moment of respite.

stages.label: Stage 15
stages.description: Under 500 metres of elevation gain. Wide Lombardian roads all the way to the finish circuits in Milan. Positioning in the final kilometres will be everything. Iljo Keisse and Luke Durbridge famously gate-crashed the sprint here in 2015, but this looks like a stage for the fast men to settle things in a bunch sprint. Paul Magnier Dylan Groenewegen Jonathan Milan Madis Mihkels Casper van Uden Ethan Vernon Pascal Ackermann Tobias Lund Andresen Matteo Malucelli Jensen Plowright Stage 15 Voghera - Milano (157.1km) After the mountains, a day of recovery — at least for the legs. Stage fifteen covers a flat 136 kilometres from Voghera into the fashion capital of Italy. Under 500 metres of elevation gain. Wide Lombardian roads all the way to the finish circuits in Milan. Positioning in the final kilometres will be everything. Iljo Keisse and Luke Durbridge famously gate-crashed the sprint here in 2015, but this looks like a stage for the fast men to settle things in a bunch sprint.

stages.label: Stage 16
stages.description: From Bellinzona — home to a trio of UNESCO-listed medieval castles — the race tackles a circuit in the Brenno valley, twice ascending the climbs to Torre (4.2 km at 6.2%) and Leontica (3 km at 8.2%). The repeated efforts will grind the peloton down. Then comes the finale: Carì, 11.2 kilometres at 8%. Long and steep, this is a climb that invites attacks from all the GC favourites. Pure climbers will be at home here. Giulio Ciccone Wout Poels Einer Rubio Reyes Jan Hirt Jonas Vingegaard Aleksander Vlasov Felix Gall Jai Hindley Enric Mas Nicolau Giulio Pellizzari Stage 16 Bellinzona - Carì (113.4km) After the rest day, the Giro crosses into Switzerland for a short but ferocious mountain stage. Only 113 kilometres, but explosive from start to finish.

stages.label: Stage 17
stages.description: Andalo is no stranger to the Giro — Steven Kruijswijk produced one of his best rides here in 2016. After an initial passage through the finish town, a loop with a descent and one final rising stretch leads riders back to the line. Giulio Ciccone Andreas Leknessund Jhonnatan Manuel Narváez Igor Arrieta Lizarraga Alberto Bettiol Damiano Caruso Wout Poels Einer Rubio Reyes Jasper Stuyven Diego Ulissi Stage 17 Cassano d Adda - Andalo (201.9km) Wednesday's stage is a long grind — 200 kilometres and 3,200 metres of climbing through northern Italy. The Passo dei Tre Termini (8.2 km at 5.9%) and the Cocca de Lodrino (8.1 km at 4.1%) are among several climbs that will sap the legs. Smaller ascents toward Roncone and San Lorenzo Dorsino add further fatigue. Andalo is no stranger to the Giro — Steven Kruijswijk produced one of his best rides here in 2016. After an initial passage through the finish town, a loop with a descent and one final rising stretch leads riders back to the line.

stages.label: Stage 18
stages.description: Two categorised climbs make life uncomfortable for pure sprinters, and the road undulates from the very start. The third-category Fastro comes mid-stage. The real finale begins at Guia, 35 kilometres out, with an intermediate sprint and an uncategorised rise. After the descent, the road barely flattens before the Muro di Ca' del Poggio (1.1 km at 12.1%) explodes into the final. This climb should not be confused with the more famous Poggio di San Remo, but a wall in the finale is a wall in the finale. The Muro di Ca' del Poggio featured in the 2024 Giro too — appearing early in the penultimate stage to Bassano del Grappa, where Tadej Pogačar took his fifth stage win of that race. Here, the summit sits just over nine kilometres from the finish in Pieve di Soligo, in the heart of Prosecco country. The finale is largely downhill. Sprinters will need to limit their losses on the climb and come back — but a bunch sprint this deep into the Giro? Don't count on it.

stages.label: Stage 19
stages.description: One hundred and fifty-one kilometres from Feltre, with the first 45 relatively manageable — before what follows makes a mockery of the word. Six climbs in total, opened by a devastating three-punch combination: the Passo del Duran (12.3 km at 8%), the short and savage Coi (5.9 km at 9.3%) and the Passo Staulanza (6.9 km at 6.1%). Above 1,700 metres at the Staulanza summit, the Passo Giau (9.7 km at 9.4%) begins. The Hors Catégorie climb is this Giro's Cima Coppi — the highest point of the race — reaching 2,233 metres above sea level. Sixty kilometres from the finish at the foot, fifty at the top. The race will be in pieces. But the stage is not over. The Passo Falzarego (11 km at 5.3%) follows, and then a long descent toward Caprile before the final sting: Piani di Pezzè, 4.9 kilometres at 9.7%, with ramps hitting 18 and 20 per cent. Short. Brutal. Decisive.

stages.label: Stage 20
stages.description: Then Piancavallo — twice. The climb is 14.3 kilometres at 7.9%. The first ascent crests with over 50 kilometres remaining, followed by a long descent and a valley run to the foot of the real summit finish. Piancavallo rings a bell for good reason. In 2020, Tao Geoghegan Hart and Wilco Kelderman settled the overall here in a head-to-head duel. The Briton won the stage and went on to claim the pink jersey in Milan. That day, they climbed Piancavallo once. On this occasion, whoever reaches the top first on the second ascent will almost certainly be the 109th Giro d'Italia's overall winner. Jonas Vingegaard Felix Gall Davide Piganzoli Giulio Ciccone Giulio Pellizzari Einer Rubio Reyes Thymen Arensman Afonso Eulálio Jai Hindley Wout Poels Stage 20 Gemona del Friuli - Piancavallo (200.9km) The penultimate stage and the final chance to overturn the GC before Rome. Two hundred kilometres from Gemona del Friuli, with the first 130 kilometres largely flat through Friuli-Venezia Giulia. A third-category climb to Clauzetto and an intermediate sprint provide the only variation.

stages.label: Stage 21
stages.description: After a neutralised roll-out, the route heads toward the Tyrrhenian coast. The Fontana dello Zodiaco near Lido di Ostia hosts an intermediate sprint; a U-turn in Ostia brings the peloton back toward central Rome. These early hours are for prosecco, photos and handshakes. The race restarts when the peloton reaches the finishing circuit in the historic centre — 9.5 kilometres, ridden eight times, identical to last year's route, when Olav Kooij sprinted to victory. Mark Cavendish, Tim Merlier and Sam Bennett have all won here before. The finishing straight is 700 metres long, slightly uphill and paved with sampietrini — Rome's small, irregular cobblestones. The circuit passes the Colosseum, the Imperial Fora, the Lungotevere, the Ara Pacis, Villa Borghese, the Circus Maximus, the Baths of Caracalla and the Castel Sant'Angelo. From the final bend, riders will glimpse St. Peter's Basilica in the distance.

stages.label: Stage 1
stages.description: No fewer than five categorized climbs are on the menu on the way to the finish in Saint-Ismier, with the toughest ascent of them all — the Côte de Rousset (8.2 km at 7.6%) — coming just twenty kilometers from the finish. Before that, the riders will already have tackled four climbs that should not be underestimated: the Col de l’Arzelier (8.6 km at 5.7%), Côte de Seyssins (2.6 km at 6%), Côte de Quaix-en-Chartreuse (2.3 km at 9.2%), and the Col de Vence (5.1 km at 6%). By the time they reach the foot of the final climb, the riders will already be seriously fatigued. Paul Seixas Juan Ayuso Pesquera Isaac Del Toro Romero Tobias Halland Johannessen Mattias Skjelmose Kévin Vauquelin Pello Bilbao Lopez de Armentia Léo Bisiaux Luke Plapp Ben Tulett Stage 1 Vizille - Saint-Ismier (146.6km) On Sunday, June 7, the riders will gather in Vizille for the start of the 78th edition of the Tour Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes. While stage races usually begin with a prologue or a sprint-friendly stage, the race immediately heads into the mountains on French soil.

stages.label: Stage 2
stages.description: Before that, the peloton will pass over the Côte de la Croix de la Cave (2.6 km at 4.5%) and the fairly long Col Robert Marchand (10.9 km at 4.4%), though these climbs are too far from the finish to cause major damage. No, any decisive attacks will most likely come on the slopes of the Côte des Baraques (4.2 km at 6.6%) or the Côte de Saint-Vidal (2 km at 7.4%), two fairly challenging climbs on the road to the finish in Le Puy-en-Velay. The Baraques summit comes just over thirty kilometers from the finish, while after the Saint-Vidal climb there are still twelve kilometers remaining, mostly on descending roads. Benoît Cosnefroy Léo Bisiaux Ben Healy Maxim Van Gils Kévin Vauquelin Kevin Vermaerke Dorian Godon Paul Seixas Mattias Skjelmose Wout van Aert Stage 2 Saint-Martin-le-Vinoux - Le Puy-en-Velay (233.5km) We can expect fireworks between the general classification contenders right from the opening stage, and stage two could also provide plenty of action. There may be fewer elevation gains on the schedule and the climbs are less demanding, but the final thirty kilometers into Le Puy-en-Velay still feature two climbs that should not be underestimated.

stages.label: Stage 3
stages.description: With the opening Tour time trial in and around Barcelona in mind, teams will be looking to fine-tune their preparation and build confidence. The course around Le Perreux is tricky, featuring two climbs toward Coutouvre and Montagny, amounting to roughly eight kilometers of climbing. The gradients may not be the steepest, but riders can certainly find themselves in trouble here. Pacing well and avoiding going too deep will be essential, because once in Montagny there are still eight extremely fast kilometers remaining to the finish in Perreux. The final kilometer also ramps up significantly, reaching gradients of up to 7%. Another important detail is that this team time trial will follow the same format as those in the Tour de France and Paris-Nice, meaning the riders’ individual times will count at the finish. For the riders with GC ambitions, this race offers very few moments of respite, because stage three is another crucial day: an undulating team time trial of just over 28 kilometers starting and finishing in Le Perreux. This will not only be a key day for the general classification, but also a final test ahead of the team time trial in the upcoming Tour de France.

stages.label: Stage 4
stages.description: To do so, they will need solid climbing legs, as the first hundred kilometers include climbs such as the Côte du Temple (5.7 km at 4.5%), Col des Pradeaux (7.2 km at 5.2%), and Col des Limites (3.7 km at 5.5%). Do not be surprised if a strong breakaway group escapes during this phase in the hope of avoiding a sprint finish. Those attackers will need to be exceptionally strong, however, because the final sixty kilometers are almost completely flat. That gives the sprint teams plenty of time to reorganize ahead of the finish in Montrond-les-Bains, a town known for its medieval castle, thermal baths, and natural riverbanks. Dorian Godon Phil Bauhaus Michael Matthews Bryan Coquard Matteo Trentin Wout van Aert Vito Braet Benoît Cosnefroy Hugo Hofstetter Andreas Kron Stage 4 Le Puy-en-Velay - Montrond-les-Bains (167.3km) After three demanding stages, the GC contenders will finally get a chance to catch their breath. Stage four is a race of two halves. The opening section still contains plenty of elevation gain and climbs (six in total), but the final fifty kilometers are virtually flat. The question is whether the sprinters will make it to the finish for a bunch sprint.

stages.label: Stage 5
stages.description: There are still two short climbs in the opening phase, and mountain points are available halfway through the stage on the Côte de Blacé (1.5 km at 4.5%), but these should not trouble the sprinters. Over the final ninety kilometers, the organizers have avoided any real obstacles, making it essentially a countdown to one last showdown between the speedsters. Wout van Aert Phil Bauhaus Bryan Coquard Dorian Godon Hugo Hofstetter Matteo Trentin Vito Braet Matevz Govekar Mathieu Kockelmann Samuel Watson Stage 5 Saint-Chamond - Parc des Oiseaux (196.3km) The sprinters are often few and far between in this race, but stage five offers the fast men an excellent opportunity to shine. The nearly 200-kilometer stage between Saint-Chamond and Villars-les-Dombes is relatively flat — certainly by Dauphiné standards. There are still two short climbs in the opening phase, and mountain points are available halfway through the stage on the Côte de Blacé (1.5 km at 4.5%), but these should not trouble the sprinters. Over the final ninety kilometers, the organizers have avoided any real obstacles, making it essentially a countdown to one last showdown between the speedsters.

stages.label: Stage 6
stages.description: Without the now-retired Cavendish, the peloton rolls out from Saint-Vulbas for an Alpine stage of two distinct halves. The first eighty kilometers are relatively straightforward, but then comes a difficult double ascent featuring the Côte de Châteland (4.3 km at 5.7%) and the better-known Col du Granier (8 km at 5.7%). It serves as a prelude to an even tougher finale. After a long flat approach, two more climbs await. First comes the ascent of the Côte d’Héry-sur-Ugine (11.3 km at 5.1%), followed by a short descent before the riders tackle the final climb (5.9 km at 7.7%) to the ski station of Crest-Voland. Differences are certain to emerge in this short but explosive finale. Paul Seixas Isaac Del Toro Romero Oscar Onley Juan Ayuso Pesquera Matteo Jorgenson Kévin Vauquelin Pello Bilbao Lopez de Armentia Ben Healy Quinn Simmons Mattias Skjelmose Stage 6 Saint-Vulbas - Crest-Voland Cohennoz (182.7km) After two transitional stages, the riders head into the mountains. The Alps host three consecutive mountain stages. Stage six is the “easiest” of the three, although the sting is definitely in the tail. The start town of Saint-Vulbas will always be remembered as the place where Mark Cavendish claimed his 35th, final, and record-breaking Tour de France stage victory two years ago.

stages.label: Stage 7
stages.description: Anyone hoping to make the early breakaway will need excellent climbing legs, because after the Col du Banchet come the Col de la Crusille (2.9 km at 4%) and the Côte de Saint-Maurice-de-Rotherens (4.9 km at 7.1%). And if the breakaway riders then hope to stay away all the way to the finish, they will still have to conquer three brutally difficult climbs. The Col de Richemond (7.7 km at 6%) is one of them, but the main focus is on the feared Col du Grand Colombier. This iconic climb appears twice on the route. The first ascent is from Culoz, the most famous side, although the riders only climb (7 km at 8.4%) to an altitude of 860 meters. The race concludes with the Col du Grand Colombier from Virieu-le-Petit, an especially brutal version of the climb (8.4 km at 10.2%). The finish line itself is “only” at 1,501 meters above sea level. Paul Seixas Isaac Del Toro Romero Matteo Jorgenson Juan Ayuso Pesquera Mattias Skjelmose Cian Uijtdebroeks Tobias Halland Johannessen Luke Plapp Luke Tuckwell Kévin Vauquelin Stage 7 Le Bridoire - Le Grand Colombier (134.2km) Stage six was already demanding, but it pales in comparison to the penultimate stage. With five categorized climbs — including one hors catégorie ascent — Saturday will once again be all about climbing. From the picturesque yet tiny village of La Bridoire, the road immediately heads uphill toward the summit of the Col du Banchet (5.3 km at 5.7%).

stages.label: Stage 8
stages.description: The Col du Pré is little more than a warm-up for the subsequent climbs of the Montée de Bisanne (11.4 km at 7.7%) and the Col des Aravis (7 km at 6.8%). Will we see an all-or-nothing attack from a GC contender on one of these climbs? Or will they save their energy for the race’s final judge and executioner: the climb to the Plateau de Solaison? This 11.3-kilometer ascent — averaging over 9% — has already served as a battleground several times in recent editions of the Dauphiné. And not only that: the climb will also feature in the upcoming Tour de France. In short, the final stage feels very much like a dress rehearsal for the upcoming Grande Boucle. Isaac Del Toro Romero Juan Ayuso Pesquera Tobias Halland Johannessen Matteo Jorgenson Paul Seixas Cian Uijtdebroeks Cristian Rodriguez Martin Mattias Skjelmose Luke Tuckwell Ben Tulett Stage 8 Beaufort - Plateau de Solaison (120.3km) If the Tour Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes has not yet been decided, it certainly will be during the relatively short but highly demanding final stage. Over just 120 kilometers, the riders must tackle four major Alpine climbs. The action begins immediately from the start, as the peloton is sent straight onto the steep slopes of the Col du Pré (6.9 km at 10.1%). This climb also featured in last year’s Tour de France.

stages.label: Stage 1
stages.description: Tadej Pogačar Romain Grégoire Mathieu van der Poel Afonso Eulálio Thibau Nys Mauro Schmid Stage 1 Sondrio - Sondrio (143.0km) The opening stage of the 2026 Tour de Suisse starts and finishes in the Italian town of Sondrio and promises immediate excitement. The route is technical and hilly, featuring several short but steep climbs that offer early opportunities for attackers and puncheurs. The general classification riders will also need to stay alert from day one to avoid losing time in the tricky finale. With 2,455 metres of elevation gain over a distance of 144 kilometres, it will be a demanding opening stage right away.

stages.label: Stage 2
stages.description: Tadej Pogačar Andrea Bagioli Mathieu van der Poel Jhonnatan Manuel Narváez Thibau Nys Mathias Vacek Stage 2 Locarno - Locarno (157.8km) The second stage of the 2026 Tour de Suisse travels through the canton of Ticino and combines a rolling route with a tricky finale. Along the way, the riders face several short climbs, giving attackers and strong classics specialists the chance to make their move. The final kilometres still feature a few punchy ascents, making a sprint from a reduced peloton a likely outcome. With 2,110 metres of elevation gain over 157.7 kilometres, another demanding and selective stage awaits.

stages.label: Stage 3
stages.description: Mathieu van der Poel Matthew Brennan Thibau Nys Magnus Cort Kaden Groves Corbin Strong Orluis Alberto Aular Sanabria Romain Grégoire Mathias Vacek Marijn van den Berg Stage 3 Bad Ragaz - Bad Ragaz (157.6km) The third stage of the 2026 Tour de Suisse is a hilly ride around Bad Ragaz that looks ideal for attackers and strong climbers. In the first part of the stage, the climbs come one after another, with several longer ascents capable of putting the peloton under serious pressure. After a selective middle section, the route becomes slightly easier towards the finale, but the repeated efforts mean that only the strongest riders are likely to contest the victory. With 2,690 metres of elevation gain over 157.9 kilometres, this will once again be a tough day in Switzerland.

stages.label: Stage 4
stages.description: Mathias Vacek Sjoerd Bax Tadej Pogačar Tobias Foss Brandon McNulty Alec Segaert Stage 4 Aarburg - Aarburg (23.7km) The fourth stage of the 2026 Tour de Suisse is an individual time trial of 23.8 kilometres in and around Aarburg. On paper, the course looks fairly fast, but it includes several rolling sections and short climbs that could disrupt the riders’ rhythm. Time trial specialists will have an excellent opportunity to gain time, while the general classification contenders must also perform strongly to avoid losing valuable seconds. With only 270 metres of elevation gain, this stage appears to favour pure power and aerodynamics.

stages.label: Stage 5
stages.description: The fifth and final stage of the 2026 Tour de Suisse will also be the queen stage of this edition. Starting from Villars-sur-Ollon, the peloton faces a brutal mountain stage featuring no less than 4,226 metres of elevation gain over 151.1 kilometres. The route consists of a succession of long climbs and technical descents, meaning the general classification could be completely reshaped here. The final climb to Villars-sur-Ollon will provide the ultimate battleground for the climbers and GC contenders, who will give everything on the final day to secure overall victory.
