stages.label: Stage 7
stages.description: The penultimate stage of the Tour of Türkiye was neutralized. There was still a stage winner, but the general classification times were recorded 15 km before the finish.

stages.label: Stage 4
stages.description: Due to the bad weather, the final lap was cancelled at the last minute, meaning the early breakaway suddenly found itself battling for the victory. The stage had originally been scheduled over 188.2 kilometres with 2,080 metres of elevation gain.

stages.label: Stage 1
stages.description: The opening stage of the 4 Jours de Dunkerque takes the peloton over 177 kilometres from Lagny-le-Sec to Laon. The route is far from flat, featuring more than 1,800 metres of elevation gain and a demanding local circuit in Laon that will be covered twice. The steep and twisting final kilometres could already create significant gaps between the favourites. As a result, this opening stage appears perfectly suited to attackers and punchers rather than pure sprinters.

stages.label: Stage 2
stages.description: Starting in Glisy, the peloton heads towards Liévin for a demanding 187-kilometre stage through the rolling terrain of northern France. Along the way, the riders face more than 1,500 metres of climbing and a constantly undulating route that could seriously shake up the race. In the finale, several short climbs and technical roads around Liévin will make for a tense battle for victory. This stage looks ideal for punchers and aggressive riders with explosive power.

stages.label: Stage 3
stages.description: Heading towards Wallers Arenberg, the peloton faces an unusual 157-kilometre stage where the cobbles take centre stage. On a local circuit around Denain and Wallers, the riders will tackle seven cobbled sectors, creating a nervous and exhausting day of racing. There are hardly any major elevation changes, but the succession of technical roads and cobblestones could lead to crosswinds, mechanical problems and surprises. This stage offers a perfect opportunity for the classics specialists to leave their mark on the race.

stages.label: Stage 4
stages.description: Between Bergues and Cassel, the riders face one of the toughest stages of this year’s 4 Jours de Dunkerque. Over 167 kilometres, the climbs come one after another, with more than 2,200 metres of elevation gain and an explosive local circuit around Cassel that must be covered five times. The mythical Rue du Tambour climb and several cobbled sections will create a brutal finale where positioning becomes crucial. This stage seems tailor-made for punchy climbers and classics specialists who excel on the cobbles.

stages.label: Stage 5
stages.description: The final stage of the 4 Jours de Dunkerque 2026 runs from Saint-Omer to Dunkirk over 181 kilometres and combines hills with a lightning-fast seaside finale. In the first part of the stage, climbs such as Mont des Cats and Boeschepe offer opportunities for attacks, but once the race reaches the coastal plains, the pace on the flat roads is expected to rise sharply. In Dunkirk, a local circuit featuring eight cobbled sectors will add extra tension to the finale. Despite the technical finish, this last stage appears to be an excellent opportunity for the stronger sprinters to end the race in style.

stages.label: Stage 1
stages.description: The sprint teams will be aiming for a bunch sprint, although attackers should also get opportunities to put the peloton under pressure along the way. After Thursday’s prologue, this stage already provides a first real test for both the general classification contenders and the fast men. Friday features the first road stage of the race. On paper, the route does not look overly difficult, but the many corners, short uphill sections and exposed roads could still make for a nervous race. The sprint teams will be aiming for a bunch sprint, although attackers should also get opportunities to put the peloton under pressure along the way. After Thursday’s prologue, this stage already provides a first real test for both the general classification contenders and the fast men.

stages.label: Stage 2
stages.description: The closing circuit features repeated ascents of the Mont des Avaloirs, the highest hill in western France. The climb, which is just over two kilometres long at an average gradient of 5.5%, will be tackled five times and could significantly shake up the general classification. A sprint from a reduced peloton appears to be the most likely outcome. On Saturday, the peloton faces a demanding stage between Aron and Pré-en-Pail-Saint-Samson. Over 215 kilometres, the riders head towards the hilly northern part of the region, where the finale is expected to provide plenty of action. The closing circuit features repeated ascents of the Mont des Avaloirs, the highest hill in western France. The climb, which is just over two kilometres long at an average gradient of 5.5%, will be tackled five times and could significantly shake up the general classification. A sprint from a reduced peloton appears to be the most likely outcome.

stages.label: Stage 3
stages.description: In the closing kilometres, the peloton completes several laps on narrow and twisting roads, ensuring that the battle for the general classification remains open until the very end. Once again, a sprint from a reduced peloton looks like the most likely scenario. The Boucles de la Mayenne conclude on Sunday with a short but nervous final stage of 147.7 kilometres between Cossé-le-Vivien and Laval. Along the way, the riders face several short climbs, but it is mainly the local circuit in Laval that promises an exciting finale. In the closing kilometres, the peloton completes several laps on narrow and twisting roads, ensuring that the battle for the general classification remains open until the very end. Once again, a sprint from a reduced peloton looks like the most likely scenario.

stages.label: Stage 1
stages.description: The stage begins relatively calmly, but the route becomes increasingly selective as the kilometres pass. Along the way, the peloton will tackle several climbs and technical sections, including the Côte de Trémouroux , the Mur de Thuin , and the steep roads around Charleroi and Thuin. Especially in the final fifty kilometres, the efforts begin to pile up, creating opportunities for attackers to make the difference. Besides the climbs, the intermediate sprints and feeding zones could also play an important tactical role in the race. The finale towards Lobbes looks particularly challenging, with short explosive climbs perfectly suited to punchers and classic-style riders. This opening stage will immediately provide a first real test for the general classification contenders. Whoever shines here will place themselves in a strong position for the remainder of the Tour de Wallonie.

stages.label: Stage 2
stages.description: During the first part of the stage, the terrain constantly rises and falls, but the real selection is expected in the finale. There, the riders face a succession of difficult climbs and technical sections, highlighted by the Côte du Fourneau Saint-Michel , a first-category climb that is certain to put the peloton under serious pressure. After that climb, a nervous finale towards Libramont-Chevigny awaits, where short ascents, fast descents and a selective finish could create significant gaps. The succession of sprints and feeding zones also adds an extra tactical dimension to the race. On paper, this second stage looks like one of the key stages of the week. Riders with general classification ambitions will not want to lose a single second here, while attackers will sense opportunities on the Ardennes roads. There is a strong chance that this stage will already reveal a clear favourite for the overall victory.

stages.label: Stage 3
stages.description: After a relatively calm opening phase, the riders are gradually faced with increasing elevation. Along the route, they will tackle climbs such as the Côte de Gériville , the Côte de la Mouline , and the Côte de Le Terme , ascents that constantly disrupt the rhythm and encourage attacks. In the second half of the stage, the climbs come in quicker succession, with the Côte de Montplanchamps standing out as the main obstacle. This first-category climb is likely to create the first major selection of the day. From there, the terrain remains rolling towards the finish in Vaux-sur-Sûre, where strong punchers and attackers may seize their opportunity. The intermediate sprints and feeding zones could also play a tactical role in this stage. With the constant ups and downs, the race is expected to become a battle of endurance where positioning and timing are crucial.

stages.label: Stage 4
stages.description: Starting from Dison, the race quickly heads into the hilly region around Jalhay and Butgenbach. The first part of the stage constantly rises and falls, preventing the peloton from ever truly settling into a rhythm. The combination of exposed roads and rolling terrain could also create nervous racing and potential crosswinds. Midway through the stage, the first major test arrives with the Côte de Ligneuville , followed by the brutal Côte de la Ferme Libert , a first-category climb that is almost certain to ignite the race. The steep gradients and relentless pace make this ascent ideal terrain for attacks from the general classification contenders. After the summit, the route remains difficult all the way to Eupen. The Côte de Ster and the continuous climbs in the finale could still create further time gaps. The finish comes after a nervous final section where positioning will be absolutely crucial.

stages.label: Stage 5
stages.description: Starting from Bassegne, the route immediately heads across rolling roads towards the famous Ardennes climbs. Early in the race, the climbs already come thick and fast, including the Côte des Forges , the Côte des Fratures , the iconic Côte de la Redoute , and the steep Côte de Cornesse . The relentless climbing is expected to gradually wear down the peloton. After the first demanding loop, the terrain remains extremely difficult towards Aubel. There, the riders face an explosive finale featuring two passages over the Côte de Stockis , a vicious second-category climb perfectly suited for late attacks. With the constant succession of climbs and descents, recovery time will be almost non-existent. The intermediate sprints and feeding zones could also play an important role during this decisive stage. Positioning will be crucial on the narrow Ardennes roads, especially approaching the final climbs.

stages.label: Stage 1
stages.description: Jasper Philipsen Olav Kooij Tim Merlier Tom Crabbe Arvid de Kleijn Milan Fretin Stage 1 Scherpenheuvel-Zichem - Scherpenheuvel-Zichem (188.4km) The Baloise Belgium Tour opens with a rolling 187.9 km stage around Scherpenheuvel-Zichem. With 1,431 metres of elevation gain and numerous short climbs, it will be far from an easy opening day. A reduced bunch sprint appears to be the most likely outcome, although attackers will certainly have opportunities along the way.

stages.label: Stage 2
stages.description: Tim Merlier Biniam Girmay Hailu Olav Kooij Arvid de Kleijn Jasper Philipsen Søren Wærenskjold Stage 2 Merelbeke - Knokke-Heist (197.6km) The second stage runs from Merelbeke-Melle to Knokke-Heist over 197.6 kilometres. With only 831 metres of elevation gain, the route is considerably flatter than the opening stage, making it an attractive prospect for the sprinters. However, coastal winds could create nervous racing. The slightly uphill finishing straight in Knokke-Heist will make timing crucial and could favour the more explosive sprinters.

stages.label: Stage 3
stages.description: Tibor Del Grosso Alex Aranburu Deba Florian Vermeersch Jenno Berckmoes Riley Sheehan Jasper Stuyven Jonas Abrahamsen Toon Aerts Carlos Canal Blanco Jasper Philipsen Stage 3 Durbuy - Durbuy (173.0km) The queen stage of this Baloise Belgium Tour takes place in the rolling terrain of Wallonia. Over 173 kilometres, the riders face 2,974 metres of elevation gain and a relentless succession of short, steep climbs. The local laps around Durbuy add another layer of difficulty, making the course highly selective. This is where the general classification contenders will need to make their move in the toughest stage of the race.

stages.label: Stage 4
stages.description: Tim Merlier Olav Kooij Jasper Philipsen Arvid de Kleijn Biniam Girmay Hailu Max Kanter Stage 4 Begijnendijk - Aarschot (182.8km) At 183.5 kilometres and with 1,361 metres of elevation gain, this stage is anything but a recovery day. The many short climbs and local laps around Aarschot could amplify the fatigue accumulated over the previous days. Not a mountain stage, but certainly a demanding transitional stage where attackers and strong classics riders may find their opportunity. A stage that should not be underestimated.

stages.label: Stage 5
stages.description: Jasper Philipsen Alex Aranburu Deba Lewis Askey Biniam Girmay Hailu Olav Kooij Matyas Kopecky Jonas Abrahamsen Jenno Berckmoes Tibor Del Grosso Lukas Kubis Stage 5 Gingelom - Hoeilaart (182.7km) The final stage from Gingelom to Hoeilaart covers 183.6 kilometres and includes 1,613 metres of elevation gain. As in the previous stages, numerous short climbs make for a selective course. The local laps around Hoeilaart, in particular, could still create significant gaps, meaning that the overall classification may remain undecided until the very last day.

stages.label: Stage 1
stages.description: The opening stage of the Tour of Slovenia runs from Velenje to Rogaška Slatina and covers 141.6 kilometers. At first glance, the route looks fairly straightforward, but it features several short and punchy climbs that could create some nervous racing in the peloton. The second half of the stage becomes more rolling, with the climb towards Jesovec pri Šmarju potentially serving as a key moment. Still, this opening stage appears to be a prime opportunity for the sprinters to make an immediate statement in Rogaška Slatina.

stages.label: Stage 2
stages.description: At 181.8 kilometers with a rolling profile, stage two of the Tour of Slovenia promises to be an open and aggressive race. The riders face a solid climb early on, while the finale also includes several tricky hills. As a result, this is unlikely to be a straightforward sprint stage, but rather an opportunity for puncheurs and attackers to make the difference towards the finish.

stages.label: Stage 3
stages.description: The third stage of the Tour of Slovenia once again features plenty of climbing. Along the way, the riders tackle two demanding ascents of more than seven kilometers at an average gradient of over six percent, with the final climb coming relatively close to the finish. That makes this stage ideal for climbers and general classification contenders looking to put their rivals under pressure. A sprint from a reduced peloton remains possible, but attacks on the final climb seem more likely.

stages.label: Stage 4
stages.description: Stage four of the Tour of Slovenia is shaping up to be a key day for the general classification. The climbs keep coming throughout the stage, with the main highlight being a demanding 12.6-kilometer ascent averaging 7.7 percent deep into the finale. After the summit, however, there is still a descent towards the finish, meaning not only the best climbers but also technically skilled descenders could seize their opportunity. Significant time gaps between the favourites therefore seem inevitable.

stages.label: Stage 5
stages.description: The final stage of the Tour of Slovenia promises one last dose of excitement. Although the 169-kilometer route is not excessively long, several short but steep climbs create a difficult and nervous course. The explosive hills in the finale in particular could split the peloton once more and offer opportunities for attackers and puncheurs. If the general classification is still undecided, the favourites will have one final chance to make the difference here.
